directive.title: 100-unit dual-LIN BLE passthrough board manufacturing plan

result.content:
# Manufacturing Plan for a Tesla Steering-Wheel Dual‑LIN BLE Passthrough Board

The steering‑wheel dual‑LIN BLE passthrough board you describe sits at the intersection of automotive robustness, RF design, and low‑volume manufacturing. Based on the current component choices—ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1U‑N8R8 and dual NXP TJA1021T/20 LIN transceivers—the design is broadly compatible with turnkey SMT assembly at 100‑unit scale, but only if you architect the supply chain and PCB design deliberately for manufacturability, test, and automotive stress. The ESP32‑S3‑WROOM modules and TJA1021 family parts are broadly available from LCSC and mainstream distributors, and can be handled by JLCPCB, PCBWay, Seeed Fusion, and MacroFab as customer‑supplied components, even when they are not in the house “basic” library.[1][7][8][2][15] For a 100‑unit run, JLCPCB and PCBWay offer the best balance of cost and friction if you do a small 5–10 unit pilot first, while MacroFab and a DigiKey/Mouser‑sourced kit plus a local assembler become more attractive if you expect to scale beyond a few hundred units or need tighter process control.[3][4][5][6][12][14][14][22][23] The optimal path is to have the MCU module, LIN transceivers, regulators, TVS/ESD, passives, and test pads assembled by an SMT service, while you reserve only bulky or specialized items such as automotive wire harnesses, steering‑wheel specific connectors, and perhaps a few through‑hole components for manual work. Careful panelization, fiducial placement, stencil design, and explicit attention to the ESP32 module’s moisture sensitivity and antenna keep‑out will be essential to avoid yield issues.[26][27][10] You should also design your ECU‑side power regulation and protection stack explicitly against load dump and ESD events, following automotive application notes from Texas Instruments and similar vendors.[17][19] Finally, you need an intentional plan for test, programming, secure‑boot provisioning, and serial‑number tracking to avoid bricking boards and to ensure reliable LIN and BLE behavior in a high‑interference steering‑wheel cavity.[17][19][25][29]

The rest of this report develops a concrete, step‑by‑step manufacturing and ordering plan, questions key assumptions in your current approach, and ends with a crisp set of recommendations for a 5–10 unit pilot and a subsequent 100‑unit build.

## System Concept and Component Strategy

### Functional and Environmental Context

The board you are designing appears intended to sit inside or immediately behind a Tesla steering wheel, bridging two independent LIN buses over BLE using an ESP32‑S3 module. LIN is widely used for steering‑wheel button packs, airbag modules, and other low‑speed in‑vehicle networks, and transceivers such as the NXP TJA1021 are explicitly designed as the physical interface between a LIN protocol controller and the bus.[2][15] The ESP32‑S3‑WROOM family gives you Wi‑Fi and BLE plus a relatively powerful dual‑core MCU in a compact RF module with integrated flash and PSRAM, reducing RF risk and assembly complexity while still allowing external LIN transceivers to interface at UART or GPIO level.[1][7]

The steering‑wheel environment imposes several non‑negotiable constraints. First, you are in a metallic, high‑EMI cavity bounded by airbag hardware and clock‑spring wiring, which will strongly affect the RF field and may detune or attenuate a PCB or chip antenna if the keep‑out is not respected.[10][20] Second, you are electrically tied into an automotive 12 V domain with possible exposure to ISO 7637‑type transients, load dump events up to 40–60 V, and substantial ESD challenges when drivers touch controls.[17][19] Third, there are real safety and legal concerns in modifying steering‑wheel assemblies, particularly if your device interferes with airbag deployment or steering controls in any way, which falls under NHTSA safety frameworks for steering systems and occupant protection.[24] These constraints must shape not only the electronics but also the physical integration, strain relief, and firmware reliability strategy.

At a high level, the core functional requirements you are implicitly targeting include dual LIN interface support, BLE connectivity to a phone or other device, low quiescent current, and robust behavior on vehicle power cycling. A compact board with an ESP32‑S3 module, two LIN transceivers, regulators, ESD/TVS protection, and perhaps a small connector for the clock‑spring harness can deliver this functionality, but only if the design is optimized for manufacturability at the 100‑unit scale and tested thoroughly before installation.

### Assessment of ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1U‑N8R8 as Core MCU Module

The ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1U‑N8R8 module is an ESP32‑S3 RF module with an external antenna connector (“1U” suffix) and 8 MB flash plus 8 MB PSRAM.[1][7] According to Espressif’s datasheet and product packaging information, these modules are designed for SMT reflow soldering and are supplied with a moisture sensitivity level (MSL) of 3, which requires dry packing and controlled exposure before reflow to avoid popcorning.[1][26] LCSC lists ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1U‑N8R8 as an in‑stock component with an orderable part number and price information, indicating ready availability in the small quantities you need.[7] Many low‑volume PCBA houses are used to placing ESP32 modules, especially in the WROOM form factor, and have no difficulty handling their relatively coarse pads and central ground slug when provided with a proper paste mask and reflow profile.[3][4][6][10]

The “1U” variant uses an external antenna connection, so its RF performance is highly contingent on antenna cable routing and external radiator placement. In a steering‑wheel environment, a small external antenna placed behind plastic trim can sometimes perform better than a heavily shielded module‑integrated antenna; however, it adds complexity in terms of cable strain relief and connector handling. If you prefer to avoid coax and associated assembly steps, you could consider the ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1‑N8R8 variant, which includes an on‑module PCB antenna and has similar memory resources.[1][7][9] This module is also widely available through LCSC and other distributors and is compatible with the same reflow process, though you must enforce a keep‑out area around the PCB antenna and maintain some distance from the steering‑wheel metal to preserve range.[1][10][20]

From a pure manufacturability and supply‑chain perspective, the ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1/1U‑N8R8 family is a sound choice for 100‑unit SMT assembly. It avoids the high risk and process complexity of placing the bare ESP32‑S3 QFN chip and external flash/PSRAM, while still enabling secure boot, flash encryption, and other advanced security features in production.[1][29] You should ensure that your PCBA partner is comfortable with reflowing high‑MSL modules and is aware of Espressif’s bake and storage requirements, but this is routine for competent assemblers.[26]

The main assumption worth questioning here is whether you truly need the S3 variant, with its neural‑network acceleration and USB interface, versus a simpler ESP32‑WROOM‑32 module that is even more common in assembly houses. Given that your use case is dual LIN plus BLE rather than computer‑vision workloads, a prior‑generation ESP32 could be sufficient. However, since you already appear architected around ESP32‑S3 and current pricing for S3 modules is competitive, sticking with ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1 or 1U is reasonable, especially if you anticipate future firmware complexity or want native USB for programming in development.[1][7][9][10] In short, as long as you design your board for the module footprint and observe RF and MSL guidance, the ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1U‑N8R8 remains a good core choice for turnkey SMT assembly.

### Assessment of Dual TJA1021 LIN Transceivers

The NXP TJA1021T/20/C,118 is a LIN 2.1/SAE J2602/ISO 17987 compliant transceiver in an SO8 package, designed as an interface between a LIN protocol controller and the physical bus.[2][15] The part supports data rates from 1 kBd to 20 kBd, low‑power sleep modes, and has automotive‑grade robustness features.[11][15] The “/20” and “C,118” suffixes refer to specific versions and packaging, typically denoting functionality such as TXD dominant time‑out and supply voltage variants, while the “CM” suffix indicates a different temperature or package option.[11][15] LCSC lists TJA1021T/20/C,118 as an in‑stock component with orderable information, indicating that you can integrate it into a JLCPCB‑style BOM by specifying the LCSC part number.[8] NXP’s documentation and Mouser’s product overview confirm its intended use in automotive LIN nodes, reinforcing its suitability for your application.[2][11][15][23]

From a manufacturing perspective, the SO8 package is straightforward for SMT assembly and is easily handled by all major PCBA vendors. There is no unusual pitch or thermal pad to worry about, and basic assembly rules such as 0.3 mm minimum spacing between SMD components and 1 mm clearance from board edges, which JLCPCB emphasizes, are easily satisfied.[12] The part’s popularity in automotive applications means that distributors such as DigiKey and Mouser carry it in volume, which gives you resilience if an LCSC‑based flow temporarily lacks stock.[8][11][22][23] 

However, your use of two transceivers per board doubles the risk that a single part shortage could stall the entire production run. It is prudent to qualify a drop‑in or near‑drop‑in alternate such as Texas Instruments’ TPIC1021 family, which implements similar LIN transceiver functionality and is often pin‑compatible or minimally different at the PCB level.[11][19] While your search results list TI TPIC1021D family as a backup, the detailed datasheet is not present in the current set; nonetheless, many PCBA houses and distributors treat TJA1021 and TPIC1021 as interchangeable options in BOM tools.[11][19] When you design the footprint, you should map it to generic SO8 dimensions and avoid vendor‑specific land patterns that would prevent alternate transceivers.

The key assumption to challenge here is whether you actually need two independent LIN channels or whether a single transceiver could be shared via multiplexing or some form of routing logic. If the steering‑wheel harness exposes two separate LIN buses that must be bridged simultaneously, dual transceivers are justified. But if the second channel is only for development or diagnostics, you could simplify the design by making it an optional population, providing unstuffed pads for the second TJA1021 but only placing one in production. This modest architectural flexibility could save cost and mitigate supply risk if transceivers become constrained.

### Overall Component Strategy and BOM Philosophy

Given this architecture—one ESP32‑S3‑WROOM module and two SO8 LIN transceivers at the core—you are in a favorable position for small‑batch SMT assembly. The remaining BOM items will be power regulation from 12 V to 5 V and 3.3 V, decoupling capacitors, TVS diodes and ESD protectors on LIN and USB/UART lines, possibly a CAN‑style 2‑pin or multi‑pin connector compatible with the steering‑wheel harness, and mechanical features such as mounting holes or strain relief anchor points. Regulators and passives should be chosen from the “basic” component libraries of your preferred PCBA vendor when possible, particularly if you use JLCPCB, whose pricing distinguishes sharply between basic and extended parts.[3][12] The JLCPCB assembly guide points out that basic resistors and capacitors cost nothing extra, while extended parts add a fixed fee per unique type, and recommends designing around library availability, stock levels, and classification to minimize costs.[12]

On this basis, your BOM strategy should be to keep the unique “extended” components limited to the ESP32 module, the LIN transceivers, any specialized TVS diodes required for automotive transients, and possibly the primary switching regulator if you choose a specific automotive‑rated part not present in the basic library. Generic passives, standard LDOs, and common logic devices can be selected from the in‑stock basic library, keeping the assembly cost per board low and reducing sourcing friction.[3][12][27] If one of your required parts is not in the PCBA house’s library at all, you can use consigned parts, shipping the reels to them, which both JLCPCB and PCBWay support for small runs.[3][4][12][28]

In summary, your chosen ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1U‑N8R8 module and dual TJA1021 transceivers are compatible with a low‑volume SMT manufacturing approach and can be supported via LCSC and mainstream distributors, as long as you plan for potential alternates and avoid over‑customization in your footprints.

## Supply Chain and Part Availability

### ESP32‑S3 Module Availability and Variants

LCSC lists ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1U‑N8R8 as an in‑stock component with pricing beginning around a few dollars per unit, depending on quantity.[7] The listing includes access to datasheets, package drawings, and pinout diagrams, which you should use to verify footprint and pad dimensions in your CAD tool.[7][1] The presence of this module in LCSC’s catalog is particularly important because JLCPCB’s parts library and BOM matching system are tightly integrated with LCSC; if you reference the LCSC part number in your BOM, the JLCPCB assembly workflow will automatically detect and match it.[3][12] The JLCPCB assembly guide explicitly recommends searching for components at jlcpcb.com/parts, filtering by assembly availability, and copying the LCSC part number into your schematic so that BOM matching is seamless.[12] This ensures that when you upload your BOM and CPL files, the system pre‑matches your ESP32 module to its inventory with a green checkmark in the component matching table.[3][12]

As a fallback, the ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1‑N8R8 (PCB‑antenna variant) is also carried by LCSC and other distributors.[7][9] This variant removes the external antenna connector and uses an on‑module PCB antenna instead, simplifying assembly by eliminating the need for external antenna cabling and connectors, but requiring more careful lay‑out of keep‑out zones and ground planes to maintain RF performance.[1][10][20] If you choose to standardize on the “1” rather than “1U” variant, you should confirm that both JLCPCB and any alternate PCBA houses can place the module reliably and that they treat it as an SMT part with proper pick‑and‑place data.

Espressif’s product packaging document notes that all their RF modules have an MSL of 3, meaning they require dry packing and have a limited floor life after being opened.[26] This is relevant to the supply chain in two ways. First, you should ensure that whichever distributor you use (LCSC, DigiKey, Mouser) ships the modules sealed in moisture barrier bags with desiccant and humidity indicators.[7][22][23][26] Second, your PCBA vendor must either open and reflow the modules within the acceptable floor life window or bake them according to Espressif’s recommendations if that window has expired.[26] Reputable vendors such as JLCPCB, PCBWay, Seeed Fusion, and MacroFab are familiar with MSL handling and will manage this internally, but you should verify their processes if you send consigned modules rather than using their house inventory.[3][4][6][14][14]

### LIN Transceiver Availability and Backups

The NXP TJA1021T/20/C,118 is listed at LCSC as TJA1021T/20/C,118 with an orderable LCSC part code and price.[8] Mouser’s product page describes NXP’s TJA1021 LIN transceivers as providing speeds from 1 kBd to 20 kBd and being pin‑to‑pin compatible with the TJA1020 and MC33662(B) series, which underscores the availability of multiple compatible options in the broader ecosystem.[11][23] The NXP datasheet (TJA1021.pdf) offers details on voltage ranges, sleep behavior, and pin assignments, all of which you should map accurately to your schematic to maintain compatibility with alternate variants such as TJA1021T/20/CM,118.[15] The “C,118” and “CM,118” suffixes usually indicate different packaging or ordering configurations but share the same electrical footprint, which is helpful for supply resilience.[11][15]

In your BOM, you can specify TJA1021T/20/C,118 as the primary part and TJA1021T/20/CM,118 as a second‑source option, both referencing the underlying NXP part number with options for substitution.[8][11][15] When interfacing with PCBA vendors and distributors, you should explicitly allow them to substitute CM for C where appropriate, provided the functionality and temperature range meet your needs. If TI TPIC1021D or related LIN transceivers are used as backups, you will need to confirm the pinout and any subtle differences in sleep/wake behavior or fail‑safe defaults.[11][19] Given that TI’s LIN transceivers are designed for similar automotive environments and that TI’s application material for automotive backlight LED controllers discusses load dump and input protection, you can expect similar robustness characteristics in TPIC1021D when combined with appropriate front‑end protection.[19]

For the supply chain, you should use LCSC for tight integration with JLCPCB if you choose that vendor, but maintain vendor‑agnostic part numbers that can be sourced from DigiKey or Mouser if needed.[8][11][22][23] In practice, for a 100‑unit run, a single reel or cut‑tape of TJA1021 parts from a major distributor will suffice, and you can ship consigned stock to your assembler if they do not already carry the part in their catalog.

### Other Key Components: Regulators, TVS/ESD, and Passives

The automotive environment demands deliberate choices for power regulation and transient protection. Texas Instruments’ system‑level ESD protection guide notes that automotive 12 V systems can require ESD diodes with working voltages up to 24 V, especially given battery transients and line spikes.[17] Similarly, TI’s application note on load dump and cranking protection for automotive LED backlight controllers explains that load dump events can push peak voltages up to 60 V, and that controllers such as LM5088‑Q1, with input ranges up to 75 V, are used in such contexts to tolerate these conditions.[19] For your design, this implies that the 12 V input from the vehicle should first be protected by a series of TVS diodes rated for automotive systems, followed by a robust buck or buck‑boost regulator that can tolerate at least 40–60 V surges and still provide a clean 5 V or 3.3 V rail to your LIN transceivers and ESP32 module.[17][19]

PCBA houses such as JLCPCB and Seeed Fusion maintain extensive libraries of regulators, but they may not include the specific automotive‑qualified devices you prefer.[3][6][12][6] The JLCPCB assembly guide suggests that extended components can be used at an extra per‑type fee and that consigned parts are possible when a particular component is not in the library.[12] For the core regulator and TVS diodes, you may accept extended parts or consignment, while selecting more generic, lower‑risk regulators from the basic library when possible for non‑critical secondary functions.[3][12][16] You should search JLCPCB’s parts database and Seeed’s Fusion library for automotive‑grade TVS diodes and regulators that meet TI’s guidance on working voltage and transient handling; if none are available, plan to ship your chosen parts to the assembler.[3][6][12][17]

Passives such as resistors, capacitors, and LEDs should be chosen from the basic component class, particularly at JLCPCB, to avoid extra fees.[12] The assembly guide notes that a basic 10 kΩ resistor has no additional cost, whereas an equivalent extended resistor can add a fixed fee of several dollars per unique extended component, which meaningfully affects the economics at 100 units.[12] You should therefore consolidate passive values where possible, using the same resistor value for similar pull‑up and pull‑down functions if it does not compromise design margins, and selecting capacitor values that exist in high‑volume in the basic library.

### Distributor Integration: DigiKey, Mouser, and Others

DigiKey and Mouser are both global, full‑service distributors that carry a wide range of electronic components from major manufacturers, including Espressif, NXP, TI, and various TVS/regulator vendors.[22][23] Mouser highlights that it partners with over 1,200 industry‑leading manufacturers and maintains a global distribution center with more than a million products in stock.[23] DigiKey likewise positions itself as a high‑availability source for both prototype and production quantities.[22] For your 100‑unit build, these distributors offer several advantages. First, they can be used as primary or backup sources for the ESP32 modules and TJA1021 transceivers if LCSC cannot deliver. Second, many local and regional assembly shops are set up to take BOMs referencing DigiKey or Mouser SKUs and handle purchasing directly, which simplifies logistics if you decide to work with a local assembler rather than an Asia‑based turnkey provider.[14][14][22][23]

In a DigiKey/Mouser plus local assembly scenario, you would typically generate a BOM with manufacturer part numbers, share it electronically, and either let the assembler order the parts or purchase them yourself and consign them. This approach is particularly attractive if you need tight control over lot traceability, which may become relevant if you aim for a more formal automotive‑like quality regime or if your volume grows beyond a few hundred units.

Overall, the supply chain for your key components is robust: ESP32‑S3‑WROOM modules and TJA1021 transceivers are available from LCSC, DigiKey, and Mouser; compatible alternates exist; and PCBA houses can accept consigned parts for any items not in their standard libraries.[3][4][5][6][7][8][11][12][22][23]

## PCBA Options and Economics for 100 Units

### JLCPCB: Integration with LCSC and Economical SMT Assembly

JLCPCB specializes in low‑cost PCB fabrication and SMT assembly, with a particularly strong offering for prototypes and small‑volume production.[3][12][27] They emphasize that their PCBA pricing starts at a flat setup fee plus a per‑solder‑joint cost, and that integrated PCB and assembly services can turn around boards in as little as a week.[3] Their assembly guide breaks down typical costs: an SMT setup fee of around eight dollars, a stencil fee, and a per‑joint price of about \(0.0017\) dollars.[12] Basic components from their library incur zero per‑type fees, while extended components add fixed charges per unique part type.[12] Since they share an inventory system with LCSC, using LCSC part numbers in your BOM allows automatic matching and reduces friction in ordering.[3][7][8][12]

The JLCPCB order flow involves uploading Gerber files, a BOM file, and a CPL (component placement list), then reviewing and confirming component choices and placement using a 3D viewer.[3][12] You can select whether to assemble the top, bottom, or both sides of the board, and choose between an economic PCBA service and a standard service.[12] For economic PCBA, JLC limits the quantity per design to 30 pieces; for larger quantities, they recommend panelization or switching to standard PCBA, which has no quantity limit.[12][27] At 100 units, you would either use panelization with multiple boards per panel under the economic service, or simply use standard PCBA with a panel design optimized for their assembly line.[27][12]

JLCPCB will place RF modules such as ESP32‑S3‑WROOM and SOIC‑8 devices like TJA1021, as long as they are present in the LCSC library or supplied as consigned parts.[3][7][8][12] They state that they support both SMT and through‑hole assembly, including wave soldering, although through‑hole components carry an additional hand‑soldering labor fee and per‑joint cost.[12] This makes them suitable for assembling the bulk of your surface‑mount components and even some connectors, while leaving specialized connectors or harnesses for manual labor either at your site or via their through‑hole service.

The main benefits of JLCPCB at 100 units are low per‑unit cost, tight integration with LCSC for sourcing, and a highly automated online ordering workflow. The primary drawbacks are the need to conform to their BOM formatting requirements, the extra cost and lead time for extended or consigned parts, and the complexity of shipping consigned components if some of your automotive‑grade parts are not in their catalog.[3][12][27] For a Tesla steering‑wheel board, where the main non‑standard items are the ESP32 module, the LIN transceivers, and automotive TVS/regulator components, this trade‑off remains favorable.

### PCBWay: Turn‑Key Assembly and Flexible Prototyping

PCBWay offers turnkey PCB assembly services similar to JLCPCB, focusing on prototyping and small‑volume production.[4][13][28] They highlight that they handle the entire process from PCB fabrication to parts sourcing and assembly, and they emphasize customer service and the ability to send progress photos during manufacturing.[4] To order an assembly, they require the usual trio of files: Gerber, BOM, and a centroid file, and they offer instant online quotes for both bare boards and assembly.[4] While they do not provide as detailed a public price breakdown as JLCPCB, they operate in a similar cost regime for small runs, particularly for 2‑layer and 4‑layer boards of modest size.[13]

PCBWay explicitly notes that, for assembly, they can handle both SMT and through‑hole, and can work with consigned parts if the customer supplies them.[4] They provide contact windows and hotlines, indicating a more hands‑on customer support model compared to JLCPCB’s primarily self‑service interface.[4][28] Their lead‑time policy indicates that fabrication turn time starts once Gerber files and other necessary documentation are received, reviewed, and approved, and similar principles apply to assembly.[28] For 100‑unit builds, they are comfortable panelizing designs and can work with mixed technology, including modules, SOIC‑8 parts, and possibly even some connectors.[4][13][28]

The flexibility of PCBWay becomes useful if you have components not readily sourced through LCSC or if you want a more consultative relationship on DFM issues. For example, if your design includes a complex RF section or automotive‑specific connectors, their engineering review and sample photos can help catch issues early.[4] They also publish examples of complex boards they have manufactured, indicating experience with multi‑layer and RF designs.[13]

Economically, PCBWay may be slightly more expensive per unit than JLCPCB for the same small batch, but the difference often shrinks in the context of shipping, communication, and problem resolution, especially for a complex automotive‑adjacent product. If your priority is smooth interaction and the ability to discuss issues with engineers, PCBWay is a strong option for both the pilot and 100‑unit run.

### MacroFab: Cloud‑Based Manufacturing and Scaling Potential

MacroFab positions itself as an intelligent electronics manufacturing platform focused on transitioning designs from prototype to larger‑scale production without changing vendors.[5][14][14] Their FabIQ platform connects BOMs to live distributor data, allowing real‑time visibility into component availability, pricing, and lead times across distributors.[14][14] They advertise no contracts or commitments and provide automatically calculated volume pricing curves for different quantities, making it easy to evaluate per‑unit costs at 10, 100, or 1,000 units.[14] 

For your application, MacroFab’s main advantages lie in supply chain transparency and the ability to scale. When you upload your BOM, their system cross‑references DigiKey, Mouser, and other distributors, showing you which components are constrained and suggesting alternates when necessary.[14][22][23][14] This is especially helpful if you want to future‑proof your design beyond 100 units, or if you anticipate that automotive‑grade TVS diodes or regulators might become hard to source. MacroFab will place RF modules, SOIC‑8 devices, and a wide range of SMT components; they also support through‑hole assembly, though at higher cost compared to SMT.[5][14][14]

The downsides of MacroFab at 100 units are primarily economic and geographic. Per‑unit prices may be higher than Asia‑based houses, especially once you factor in overhead and North‑American labor costs. However, shipping and communication are often simpler, and many developers value the ability to iterate quickly without the friction of international logistics. If you foresee evolving this product into a commercial offering with regulatory or quality requirements beyond hobbyist projects, MacroFab’s infrastructure and documentation may be worth the extra cost compared to purely cost‑optimized services.

### Seeed Fusion: One‑Stop PCB and Assembly with Moderate Volumes

Seeed Studio’s Fusion PCB assembly service offers one‑stop PCB manufacturing and assembly with a focus on speed and cost effectiveness.[6][6] They advertise turnaround times as fast as seven days for assembled boards and promote low starting costs, such as around 4.90 dollars for ten bare PCBs in simple configurations.[6] Like JLCPCB and PCBWay, Seeed Fusion incorporates a parts library and supports turnkey sourcing, consigned parts, and mixed technology assembly.[6][6]

Seeed Fusion’s strengths are its integration with Seeed’s broader ecosystem (including Open Parts Library and community projects) and its willingness to handle small prototype quantities and moderate volumes. For a 5–10 unit pilot run, they are a viable alternative to JLCPCB and PCBWay, particularly if you are already familiar with their platform. However, at 100 units, their per‑unit cost may not be as low as JLCPCB if you heavily optimize for their parts library and sourcing model.[3][6][12][6]

Seeed is comfortable placing modules such as ESP32‑WROOM, and they have experience with IoT and RF designs, which makes them a good option if your board’s RF aspects are complex or if you want design feedback in addition to manufacturing.[6][10][6] As with other vendors, they require Gerber, BOM, and pick‑and‑place files, and they will send you order updates during manufacturing.

### Local Assembly with DigiKey/Mouser Kit

An alternative path is to procure bare PCBs (from JLCPCB, PCBWay, or a local board house), purchase all components from DigiKey or Mouser, and then have a local assembly shop place the components using your BOM and pick‑and‑place data.[22][23][14] This approach decouples PCB fabrication from assembly and gives you full control over component sourcing, which is useful if you want strict traceability and the ability to qualify each lot of automotive‑grade components.

Local assembly can be more expensive per unit than offshore turnkey services, but it offers significant convenience for debugging, rework, and communication. If something goes wrong in assembly, you can visit the shop, examine boards in person, and iterate quickly. Many local shops are accustomed to placing RF modules, SOIC‑8 devices, and mixed SMT/THT components, though their familiarity with automotive harness connectors will vary.

Economically, at 100 units, local assembly costs will depend heavily on your region, but they are likely to be higher than JLCPCB or PCBWay unless you negotiate favorable rates. However, if you view this product as a stepping stone to a more formal automotive‑grade product, the discipline imposed by local assembly and standard distributor sourcing may be beneficial.

### Comparative Assessment and Best Fit at 100 Units

For your specific case—a one‑off automotive accessory with a target of about 100 units and potential for small revisions—the most balanced option is to use JLCPCB or PCBWay for both the pilot run and the 100‑unit build. JLCPCB offers the lowest per‑unit costs if you align your BOM with their basic and extended parts model, while PCBWay offers slightly more hands‑on support and flexibility at a modest cost premium.[3][4][12][27][28]

MacroFab and local assembly with DigiKey/Mouser parts are attractive if you expect to commercialize the design at higher volumes or if you require more formal supply‑chain management. Seeed Fusion sits in the middle, with good RF experience and integrated services but less aggressive pricing than JLCPCB in many cases.[5][6][14][14][6]

In any case, the key is to run a 5–10 unit pilot to validate assembly, RF performance, and in‑vehicle behavior before committing to a 100‑unit build. This pilot should use the exact same BOM and assembly flow you intend for the final run to minimize surprises.

## Assembly Strategy and Design for Manufacturability

### SMT Assembly Scope and What to Hand‑Install

To avoid hand‑soldering 100 units, you should maximize the scope of SMT assembly and reserve manual work only for those elements that are impractical or prohibitively expensive to assemble in an automated line. The core items that should absolutely be included in SMT assembly are the ESP32‑S3‑WROOM module, both TJA1021 transceivers, all power regulation components (switching regulator, LDO, associated inductors and diodes), all decoupling and bulk capacitors, resistors, crystal or oscillator components if any, and all ESD/TVS devices on LIN and USB/UART lines.[1][2][15][17][19] Test pads for programming, LIN, and power rails should also be implemented as SMT pads or exposed copper and will naturally be created as part of the bare PCB fab.

Connectors are a nuanced case. Many automotive harness connectors are through‑hole or require mechanical reinforcement beyond what an SMT house typically does. While some surface‑mount headers and micro‑connectors can be assembled by JLCPCB or PCBWay, large automotive‑style connectors or custom harness interfaces may be better hand‑installed, especially if you need to fine‑tune their orientation relative to the steering‑wheel cavity. The JLCPCB assembly guide notes that through‑hole components incur additional hand‑soldering fees, which may become economically unattractive at 100 units for complex connectors.[12] As a result, you may decide to have the boards delivered with all SMT components populated but no steering‑wheel harness connectors; you would then solder the connectors and integrate cable harnesses manually or via a separate harness assembly process.

If some specialized components are unavailable in the PCBA house’s library, you can still have them SMT‑assembled using consigned stock, provided you accept some extra logistics and possibly higher per‑component costs.[3][4][12][28] This is particularly relevant for automotive TVS diodes or regulators that are not widely used in consumer electronics.

In short, your SMT assembly scope should cover everything except perhaps the steering‑wheel harness connectors and any large through‑hole mounting hardware. Hand installation should be limited to connectors, rare mechanical parts, and last‑minute modifications discovered during pilot testing.

### Panelization, Fiducials, and Stencil Considerations

At 100 units, panelization is highly recommended to improve assembly efficiency and lower per‑unit costs. JLCPCB’s panelization guide explains that grouping multiple PCBs into a panel allows them to be assembled simultaneously, increasing production throughput and reducing waste, which in turn lowers the cost per unit.[27] For quantities above 50 boards, they strongly recommend panelization, either as customer‑defined arrays or as fabrication‑side panelization.[27] You should therefore design a panel that places multiple copies of your steering‑wheel board in an array that fits within the assembler’s panel size limits—often around 250 mm by 250 mm for economic services.[12][27]

The panel should include global fiducials (copper pads with solder mask openings) at three non‑collinear locations for the pick‑and‑place machine, as well as local fiducials near fine‑pitch components if necessary. Even though the ESP32 module and TJA1021 are not especially fine‑pitch, local fiducials near the ESP32 module can improve placement accuracy. JLCPCB’s assembly guidelines mention that adequate spacing between components and between components and board edges is important; specifically, they encourage at least 0.3 mm between SMD components and 1 mm from the edges, which you should implement in your design.[12] These clearances also help you route differential pairs or RF traces without crowding components, which is particularly important if you use USB or external antenna routing.

Stencil design is critical for the ESP32 module’s large ground pad. The deep‑dive ESP32‑S3 PCB design video demonstrates that segmenting the ground pad’s top paste layer into multiple apertures reduces solder voiding and improves reflow quality.[10] Espressif’s own reference designs also show segmented paste patterns for the module’s central pad.[1][10] When generating your paste layer, you should follow these recommendations, avoiding a solid paste area under the entire pad. You may include small vias in the pad to conduct heat to the other side of the board, though the video notes that this can cause some via leakage unless the vias are filled with epoxy.[10] For your small board, you can leave the vias unfilled if necessary, accepting a minor amount of solder wicking to the back side.

### RF Layout and Antenna Considerations

If you use ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1‑N8R8 with a PCB antenna, you must strictly observe the manufacturer’s requirements for antenna keep‑out and ground plane cutbacks.[1][20] The steering‑wheel cavity is mostly metal, so any ground plane or copper directly under or around the antenna area will impair performance. Espressif reference designs and community discussions on ESP32‑S3 RF component selection emphasize the importance of a clear, ungrounded area beneath the antenna and minimal proximity to large metal structures.[1][20] The ESP32‑S3 PCB design deep dive further illustrates the need to treat RF traces and antenna feed lines carefully, ensuring proper impedance and small clearances between RF traces and nearby copper pours to achieve the required characteristic impedance without excessive width.[10]

If you stick with the “1U” external‑antenna module, your RF design shifts to the coax and external antenna. You must still consider the antenna’s location relative to the steering wheel structure, ideally choosing a location behind plastic trim where the radiating element has a semi‑clear line of sight to the cabin. The coax connector and cable should be strain‑relieved, especially since the steering‑wheel assembly flexes as the wheel turns. SMT assembly of the coax connector is straightforward for JLCPCB and PCBWay if the connector is in their library or supplied as a consigned part, but the actual antenna installation in the vehicle will be a manual process.

You should question the assumption that BLE performance will be adequate inside the steering‑wheel cavity without empirical testing. During the 5–10 board pilot, you must conduct in‑vehicle RF measurements, checking connection stability and throughput with the wheel at various positions. If performance is marginal, you may need to switch from PCB to external antenna or adjust antenna placement.

### MSL and Reflow Process for ESP32 and Other ICs

Espressif’s packaging documentation states that all their modules have an MSL of 3 and must therefore be dry‑packed with desiccants and humidity indicators.[26] Once opened, they can be exposed to ambient conditions only for a limited floor life before they must either be reflowed or baked to drive out absorbed moisture.[26] If this is not respected, the modules can suffer internal delamination and cracking during reflow, leading to latent reliability issues. Reputable PCBA vendors handle MSL requirements as a matter of course. You should nonetheless inform your vendor that the ESP32 modules are MSL 3 and ask them to confirm that they will bake or reflow within the allowed floor life.

Other ICs such as the TJA1021 transceivers are also likely to have MSL ratings, though typically less stringent than RF modules.[15] Ensure that your BOM and documentation identify any MSL‑sensitive components, especially if you supply them as consigned parts. For example, if you purchase a reel of ESP32‑S3‑WROOM modules from LCSC and ship it to PCBWay, you should ensure that the reel is still sealed or has been handled under controlled conditions to avoid MSL violations.[7][26]

### Mixed Technology: SMT vs THT

Your design will likely be dominated by SMT components, which is ideal for modern electronics and small‑batch assembly. The Queenems analysis of SMT versus THT explains that SMT offers better electrical performance at high frequencies because of shorter leads, and it enables smaller and denser designs.[16] THT, by contrast, offers superior mechanical strength and is often used for components subject to physical stress or high voltages.[16] Mixed technology, combining SMT for logic and THT for power and mechanical components, is common and requires assembly lines that can handle both reflow and wave soldering.[16] 

In your case, SMT should be used for the ESP32 module, LIN transceivers, regulators, TVS diodes, and passives to keep the board compact and assembly cost low.[1][2][16] THT is best reserved for large connectors that need mechanical strength and for any toggle switches or mechanical fixtures that may be accessible to the user. Both JLCPCB and PCBWay support mixed‑technology assembly, but they charge extra for THT hand soldering, which suggests that you should minimize THT components unless necessary.[3][4][12][16]

By designing the PCB to rely heavily on SMT and using THT only where mechanical robustness is paramount, you balance manufacturability, cost, and in‑vehicle reliability.

## Test, Programming, and Security Provisioning

### Test Strategy: Pogo‑Pin Jig and Functional Tests

Given that your board will be embedded in a steering‑wheel assembly and not easily accessible after installation, you need a robust manufacturing test strategy. The most practical approach is to expose a row or cluster of test pads on the PCB that can be accessed via a pogo‑pin test jig. The EEVblog discussion on pogo pin test jigs describes common approaches, such as mounting pogo pins directly in through‑holes on a tester PCB or using two PCBs separated by spacers to hold a field of pins.[21] The conversation recommends respect for pin head shapes to ensure appropriate contact and suggests that thicker PCB material (for example, 3.2 mm instead of 1.6 mm) improves mechanical stability when using many pogo pins.[21]

Your test jig should make contact with pads for power, ground, UART or USB for programming, both LIN channels (LIN and ground), and at least one GPIO to exercise BLE advertisement or provide a test trigger. A typical flow might involve plugging the board into the jig, powering it from a lab supply, programming the ESP32, running LIN loopback and BLE tests, and logging results to a database or spreadsheet.

For LIN testing, the NI community discussion on LIN back‑to‑back loopback suggests linking two LIN ports to each other and verifying that data is correctly communicated between them.[25] In your context, you can either use the two LIN channels on the board interconnected through an external harness or use a dedicated LIN master tool to talk to each channel in turn.[25] The NI example emphasizes creating frame input and frame output sessions and using schedules to define when frames are sent, which you can adapt conceptually to your own automated test software.[25] For a simpler approach, you could have the ESP32 implement a LIN loopback test internally by transmitting known frames and measuring responses through the transceivers.

BLE testing can be as simple as scanning for advertisements using a phone or test laptop and logging RSSI and connection success. For production, you may want a semi‑automated script that uses a BLE dongle to connect, exchange a small data packet, and report success or failure.

### Programming and Provisioning Flow

Because your device will be deployed in vehicles and potentially updated over BLE or Wi‑Fi, you should adopt a deliberate secure‑boot and flash encryption strategy. An Espressif forum discussion outlines a workflow for secure boot and encryption in production programming, detailing steps such as configuring the project to generate signed binaries, generating encryption keys, encrypting partitions, merging binaries, and burning eFuses.[29] In one variant of the workflow, the app is compiled once, signed, and then encrypted offline. On the production line, a merged binary is written to flash, secure boot keys and flash encryption keys are burned into eFuses, and other security‑related eFuses are set.[29] In another approach, flash encryption keys are generated on device, and only NVS partitions are encrypted offline.[29]

For your product, you should decide early whether secure boot and flash encryption are necessary. If you are concerned about reverse engineering or malicious firmware modification—reasonable concerns for a vehicle‑attached device—you should at least enable secure boot. The production programming workflow could look like this: during development, configure your ESP‑IDF project to use secure boot with a known signing key and generate signed binaries. At production time, use a fixture that can flash the merged binary (bootloader, partition table, app, and optional encrypted NVS), then burn the secure boot eFuse and other security‑related fuses using a script driven by espsecure and espefuse utilities.[29] After fuses are burned, reboot the board and allow it to complete any one‑time flash encryption or key derivation processes if enabled.

It is crucial to test this provisioning flow extensively on pilot units, because mistakes in eFuse programming can permanently brick modules. You should implement a procedure that checks each board’s boot logs after provisioning to confirm that secure boot and encryption are active and that the board still boots reliably.

### Serial Numbering and Traceability

For quality control and debugging in the field, each board should have a unique serial number, which you can encode in silkscreen, laser marking, or a printed label. You can also store a serial number or unique ID in a reserved NVS partition on the ESP32, linking it to your production database. During the test process, your jig software can read the module’s MAC address and associate it with your serial number and test results. This traceability will be invaluable when diagnosing failures reported by users.

The test logs should record power‑on behavior, LIN loopback performance, BLE connection quality, and any anomalies observed during provisioning. In the long term, this data will help you identify systemic issues in assembly or design.

## Environmental and Regulatory Risks

### RF Behavior in the Steering‑Wheel Cavity

The steering‑wheel cavity is a challenging RF environment. The presence of the airbag module, metal wheel frame, and other hardware creates a reflective, lossy enclosure that can significantly attenuate BLE signals. PCB or chip antennas are particularly sensitive to their immediate environment; if mounted too close to metal, their impedance can shift, reducing radiated power or altering radiation patterns.[10][20] Espressif’s antenna design guidelines and the ESP32‑S3 RF deep dive demonstrate that proper ground clearance and placement relative to the board edge are essential.[1][10][20]

If you choose ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1 with a PCB antenna, you should place the module at the edge of the board and route the board so that the antenna overhangs or is adjacent to open air rather than buried in metal. You may need to cut a window or slot in any mounting bracket to avoid putting metal directly under the antenna. If you use ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1U with an external antenna, you can route the antenna cable to a more favorable location behind plastic trim, but you must ensure that the cable is not pinched or excessively bent when the steering wheel turns.

In your pilot run, you must explicitly test BLE performance under realistic conditions—vehicle powered, wheel turned to extreme positions, driver using typical smartphone positions. If performance is marginal, you may have to adjust antenna type or placement, even if that means redesigning the board to use the other ESP32 variant.

### Automotive Electrical Environment: ESD and Load Dump

Automotive 12 V systems subject your design to a combination of ESD, surge, and load dump challenges that far exceed those of typical consumer electronics. TI’s system‑level ESD protection guide notes that in 12 V battery systems, an ESD diode with a working voltage of 24 V is often required to account for battery voltage variations and spikes.[17] When ESD strikes occur, ESD diodes create a low‑impedance path to ground, diverting current and limiting peak voltage and current to protect downstream ICs.[17] For high‑speed I/O such as USB 3.1, the guide also notes that ESD devices must have very low capacitance (less than 3 pF) to maintain signal integrity, but LIN and other low‑speed lines can tolerate higher capacitances.[17]

Load dump, described in TI’s application note on automotive backlight controllers, occurs when a battery is suddenly disconnected from the alternator, causing a large transient across the vehicle power system.[19] Transient peaks can reach up to 60 V, and controllers such as LM5088‑Q1 are designed to survive inputs up to 75 V during such events.[19] TI discusses approaches such as using supercapacitors, bridge rectifiers, and transient disconnect circuits to shield sensitive electronics from these events.[19]

For your steering‑wheel board, which taps into 12 V or a derived power feed through the clock‑spring harness, you must assume exposure to similar transients. This implies that your power input stage should include a suitably rated TVS diode and possibly a series protection element, followed by a high‑voltage tolerant regulator. Even if you derive your board power from a branch that is already protected inside the vehicle, you cannot safely assume that its transient behavior is benign without documentation from Tesla, which you likely do not have.

You should challenge any assumption that a consumer‑grade buck converter or LDO is adequate for this environment. Automotive‑rated parts, ideally AEC‑Q100 qualified, are strongly recommended. If your chosen PCBA vendor does not stock those parts in their library, consignment via DigiKey or Mouser is the right approach.[17][19][22][23]

### Mechanical and Wiring Risks: Connector Strain and Harness Labor

The steering‑wheel assembly moves constantly, and the clock‑spring harness transmits both power and LIN signals across this movement. Any additional harnesses or connectors you introduce must be designed for flex, vibration, and strain. SMT connectors without adequate strain relief can rip off the board under repeated stress. THT connectors, anchored through the board with mechanical pins, are generally better suited for this environment.[16]

You need to question the assumption that you can simply hang a small board and connector bundle inside the steering wheel without considering mounting and strain relief. The board should have mounting holes and be bolted or clipped to a stable structure, with cable ties or strain relief features to secure harnesses. The mechanical design should be validated in a prototype steering‑wheel environment before production.

Cable harness labor is also significant. Even if you have boards fully assembled by a PCBA house, the process of attaching connectors to wires, crimping terminals, and assembling harnesses may be done manually or through a specialized harness manufacturer. At 100 units, you might hand‑assemble harnesses, but if this design scales, you would engage a harness vendor. For now, you should design your board connectors and harness lengths to be as simple and repeatable as possible.

### Firmware Risk: Bricking and Safety Behavior

With secure boot and flash encryption enabled, misprogramming eFuses can permanently brick boards, which is costly at 100 units and potentially dangerous if a bricked device fails in a state that interferes with steering‑wheel controls.[29] You must have a clear provisioning process and test it thoroughly. Another firmware‑related risk is that a bug in your code could disrupt LIN communications, causing steering‑wheel buttons or other functions to fail or misbehave. Even if you are only “passthrough‑ing” LIN traffic, you should implement watchdogs and fail‑safe behaviors that default to transparent pass‑through if the ESP32 resets or hangs.

You should also question the assumption that it is acceptable to deploy a non‑fail‑safe device into the steering‑wheel LIN bus. Even if steering‑wheel LIN nodes are not safety‑critical in the same way as airbags or braking systems, driver interface failures can still be hazardous and may have legal implications.

### Legal and Safety Considerations

Modifying steering‑wheel assemblies and integrating devices into vehicle control systems raises legal and safety questions. While your device is not directly controlling steering torque or airbag deployment, its presence inside the steering wheel and on LIN buses associated with steering‑wheel controls implicates regulatory frameworks, including NHTSA’s Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards (FMVSS) for steering controls and occupant protection.[24] A recent NHTSA proposal to amend FMVSS No. 204 illustrates that the agency continues to refine regulations around steering control rearward displacement and occupant safety.[24]

You are unlikely to be directly bound by OEM‑level FMVSS certification for a small aftermarket product, but you should still behave as if the steering‑wheel environment is safety‑sensitive. This means avoiding mechanical modifications that could affect airbag deployment or steering integrity, ensuring that your device does not interfere with OEM wiring or controls, and being transparent with users that the device is experimental and may not be approved by the vehicle manufacturer.

If you ever consider commercializing this product beyond a small enthusiast run, you would need legal counsel and potentially automotive functional safety experts to evaluate ISO 26262 implications and liability.

## Practical Recommendations and Order Plan

### Recommended Parts and Design Approach

Based on the analysis, your current core selection is workable with some refinements. For the MCU module, stick with ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1‑N8R8 if you want the simplest assembly and are willing to carefully manage antenna placement, or use ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1U‑N8R8 if you prefer an external antenna positioned in a more RF‑friendly location.[1][7][9][10][20] Both are available from LCSC and can be integrated in JLCPCB’s BOM system using their part numbers.[7][12] For the LIN transceivers, specify NXP TJA1021T/20/C,118 as the primary choice, with TJA1021T/20/CM,118 and TI TPIC1021 family parts as alternates, ensuring that your footprint and schematic support pin‑compatible variants.[2][8][11][15][19]

Select automotive‑grade TVS diodes and regulators that meet TI’s load dump and ESD guidelines, sourcing them from DigiKey or Mouser if necessary and providing them as consigned parts to your PCBA vendor.[17][19][22][23] Use JLCPCB’s or your chosen assembler’s basic component library for resistors, capacitors, and other simple passives to keep costs down.[3][12] Design the PCB with ample spacing between components, global and local fiducials, and a panelization scheme suitable for 100 units.[12][27]

Add a dedicated row of test pads for power, ground, UART/USB, and both LIN channels, to be used with a pogo‑pin jig for programming and testing.[21][25] Reserve NVS partitions and eFuse configuration for secure boot and flash encryption if you choose to implement them, following Espressif’s recommended production workflows.[29]

### Pilot Run: 5–10 Unit PCBA

For the first pilot batch of 5–10 units, you should treat this as a full dress rehearsal of your final production flow, not a hand‑assembled experiment.

You should order bare PCBs and SMT assembly from a vendor such as JLCPCB or PCBWay. Provide Gerber, BOM with LCSC part numbers where possible, and CPL files. For components not in their library, such as specific automotive TVS diodes or regulators, you can either choose close library equivalents for the pilot or consign the exact parts.[3][4][12][28] Include the ESP32 module and TJA1021 transceivers in the SMT assembly scope, along with all passives and regulators.[1][2][8][15][16]

You should design and construct a simple pogo‑pin test jig using one or two PCBs and spacers, as described in the EEVblog thread.[21] The jig should connect to a USB‑UART adapter and a power supply, and optionally to a LIN master or your own LIN loopback harness. You should write a basic test script that programs each board, verifies LIN communication through a loopback or back‑to‑back setup, and checks BLE advertisements using a standardized test procedure.[25][29]

Once you receive the pilot boards, test them both on the bench and in a steering‑wheel mock‑up or actual vehicle. Pay particular attention to BLE performance, power behavior during vehicle cranking, and LIN communication stability. Adjust your design as necessary based on these findings.

### 100‑Unit Production Run

Once the pilot batch is validated, you can proceed to a 100‑unit production run using the same PCBA vendor. You should:

Finalize your panelization, ensuring it is optimized for their assembly process.[27] Upload the final Gerber, BOM, and CPL files to the PCBA vendor’s platform, confirming component matching and placement in the online viewer.[3][4][12]

Source a sufficient quantity of ESP32 modules, TJA1021 transceivers, automotive TVS diodes, and regulators. Prefer using LCSC for parts integrated with JLCPCB; use DigiKey or Mouser as backup sources.[7][8][11][22][23]

Decide whether to have connectors assembled by the PCBA house or to solder them manually. For large automotive connectors, manual installation may be more reliable and cost‑effective.[12][16]

Implement your secure‑boot and provisioning flow using the test jig, following the Espressif secure boot and encryption workflow.[29] Log serial numbers, MAC addresses, and test results for each unit.

Ship completed boards to your integration site, where you will attach harnesses, mount boards in steering wheels, and perform vehicle‑level testing.

### Direct Owner‑Level Recommendation

For the board you are building, the best practical path is:

Use the ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1‑N8R8 or 1U‑N8R8 module and two NXP TJA1021T/20 LIN transceivers per board. Both are readily available from LCSC and mainstream distributors and are fully compatible with SMT assembly.[1][2][7][8][11][15]

Have JLCPCB or PCBWay assemble all SMT parts: the ESP32 module, both LIN transceivers, regulators, TVS/ESD parts, and all passives, using their turnkey service with LCSC‑linked BOM entries and consigned parts only where necessary.[3][4][7][8][12][27][28]

Reserve hand‑soldering only for steering‑wheel harness connectors and any large through‑hole mechanical parts, to avoid paying assembly houses for high‑labor THT operations on small batches.[12][16]

Run a 5–10 unit pilot batch first, with a proper pogo‑pin test jig and in‑vehicle testing to validate BLE range, LIN behavior, and power robustness. Only after validating this pilot should you commit to a 100‑unit run using the same PCBA flow.[10][21][25]

Design for panelization, RF keep‑out, ESD/load‑dump protection, and secure‑boot provisioning from the start, so you do not need to re‑layout the board before scaling.[17][19][26][27][29]

If you follow this plan, you can obtain 100 assembled boards without hand‑soldering the critical components, while respecting automotive constraints and keeping per‑unit costs reasonable.

## Conclusion

By grounding the hardware architecture in proven components such as the ESP32‑S3‑WROOM module and NXP TJA1021 LIN transceivers, and by aligning the BOM with the capabilities and libraries of PCBA vendors like JLCPCB and PCBWay, you can move from prototype to a 100‑unit production run without resorting to hand‑soldering critical SMT parts.[1][2][3][4][7][8][11][12][15] The supply chain for these core components is robust via LCSC, DigiKey, and Mouser, and alternates such as ESP32‑S3‑WROOM‑1 versus 1U and TI TPIC1021D provide resilience against shortages.[7][8][11][19][22][23] 

The essential manufacturing strategy is to maximize SMT assembly scope, leaving only steering‑wheel harness connectors and perhaps a few mechanical pieces for manual installation, and to implement panelization, fiducials, and proper stencil design to ensure high yields.[12][16][26][27] Attention to RF layout in the steering‑wheel cavity, automotive ESD and load dump protection, and mixed SMT/THT assembly is critical to ensuring reliable operation in a harsh automotive environment.[10][17][19][20] At the same time, a robust test and provisioning flow—anchored in pogo‑pin jigs, LIN loopback tests, BLE connectivity checks, and Espressif’s secure‑boot and encryption workflows—will minimize the risk of shipping bricked or unreliable devices.[21][25][29]

You should also remain cognizant of legal and safety considerations around modifying steering‑wheel systems, and design your product to be as non‑intrusive and fail‑safe as possible in that context.[24] Running a focused pilot of 5–10 units, assembled using the same turnkey SMT service intended for the 100‑unit run, will give you the empirical data needed to refine the design and manufacturing plan before scaling.

For the owner, the short operational summary is straightforward: keep the chosen ESP32‑S3 module and dual LIN transceivers, design around JLCPCB or PCBWay’s SMT assembly capabilities with LCSC‑linked parts, have them assemble everything SMT including RF modules and LIN chips, hand‑install only the steering‑wheel connectors, validate with a 5–10 unit pilot using a pogo‑pin test jig in a real steering wheel, then repeat the same assembly flow for 100 units. This approach avoids hand‑soldering, respects the automotive environment, and uses supply chains and manufacturing partners that are well‑suited to your project’s scale.
